---
title: "Commitments"
subtitle: "Bind a value without revealing it. The other half of the privacy story."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

Merkle proofs (the [previous chapter](02-merkle-trees.qmd)) prove a value is
in a set. But how do you prove you committed to a value at time T₀, without
revealing the value, AND prevent yourself from changing it at time T₁?

This is the **commitment** problem. It's everywhere in privacy chains:
sealed bids, anonymous voting, invoice hashes, vote counts.

## What this chapter will cover

- Hash commitments: `commit = hash(value, salt)`
  - Why salt? Without it, the commitment is brute-forceable for low-entropy values
- Pedersen commitments: `commit = a·G + b·H` (homomorphic — you can add commitments without opening them)
- When to use which: hash for "I want to prove I knew X at T₀," Pedersen for "I want to prove sum-of-votes without revealing each vote"
- Aleo's built-in commitment families: BHP, Pedersen
- The on-chain commitment + off-chain encrypted blob pattern (already foreshadowed in `architecture-payments.md` if you came from `aleo-skills`)

## Interactive viz planned

A "commit-reveal" timeline:
1. T₀: user picks a value, commitment is published
2. T₁: someone tries to forge a different value with the same commitment
3. The viz shows the cryptographic obstruction (collision-resistance)

Plus a Pedersen homomorphism demo: two committed values, add the commitments,
show that opening the sum equals the sum of the openings.

## Real-world payoff

NullPay (the example invoice app referenced throughout this book) uses
commitments to bind invoice JSON to its on-chain row. Every privacy-payments
app needs this primitive. Once you understand it, you can read NullPay's
[`pay_invoice`](../04-real-systems/04-nullpay-walkthrough.qmd) and immediately
see what's happening.
